title: Cybersecurity & VAPT Quiz
description: Expert-level questions on web security, penetration testing, cloud security, cryptography, OWASP Top 10, and DevSecOps.

questions.question: What does CIA stand for in information security, and which property does ransomware primarily violate?
options: A. CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability; ransomware violates Availability (and Confidentiality)
B. CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Accountability; ransomware violates Integrity
C. CIA = Control, Identify, Analyze; ransomware violates Control
D. CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability; ransomware only violates Integrity

questions.question: Which OWASP Top 10 (2021) category moved to position #1 and includes IDOR and missing function-level access control?
options: A. A03 — Injection
B. A01 — Broken Access Control
C. A02 — Cryptographic Failures
D. A05 — Security Misconfiguration

questions.question: What makes a parameterized query (prepared statement) prevent SQL injection?
options: A. It encrypts user input before passing it to the database
B. It validates input length and character type before query execution
C. It separates SQL code from user data — the database driver ensures user data is never interpreted as SQL commands
D. It runs queries in a sandboxed environment with no write permissions

questions.question: Which XSS type stores the malicious payload in the database, affecting every user who views that content?
options: A. Reflected XSS
B. DOM-based XSS
C. Blind XSS
D. Stored (Persistent) XSS

questions.question: What is SSRF and why is it critical when targeting cloud infrastructure?
options: A. Server-Side Request Forgery — attacker makes the server fetch internal URLs; in cloud, can reach the metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254) and steal IAM credentials
B. SQL Server Request Failure — causes DB downtime
C. Session-Stealing Request Forwarding — hijacks authenticated sessions
D. Secure Socket Request Failure — bypasses TLS validation

questions.question: What does the Nmap '-sS' (SYN scan) flag do differently from a full TCP connect scan?
options: A. Sends only SYN packets and waits for all responses before continuing
B. Sends SYN, receives SYN-ACK, then sends RST — half-open, often avoids connection logging
C. Completes the full 3-way handshake, making it undetectable
D. Scans all 65535 ports sequentially using SYN packets

questions.question: In CVSS v3.1, a vulnerability with AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H scores 10.0. What does 'S:C' (Scope: Changed) mean?
options: A. The vulnerability can only be exploited in a changed network environment
B. The attack changes the network scope from local to remote
C. Exploiting the vulnerability impacts resources beyond the vulnerable component's authorization scope
D. Scope changed means additional user interaction is required

questions.question: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption, and why is TLS hybrid?
options: A. Symmetric uses public/private keys; asymmetric uses one shared key. TLS uses asymmetric for everything.
B. Symmetric is only for files; asymmetric is only for network. TLS uses asymmetric for both.
C. They are identical in modern implementations — TLS uses whichever the server prefers
D. Symmetric (same key, fast) encrypts bulk data; asymmetric (key pair, slow) is used in TLS handshake to establish a session key — TLS uses both

questions.question: A web app hashes passwords with unsalted MD5. What attack is most effective?
options: A. Rainbow table attack — precomputed MD5 hashes for millions of common passwords can crack unsalted hashes instantly
B. Brute force with a password list
C. MITM attack to intercept the plaintext password
D. SQL injection to bypass the password check

questions.question: What does the Content-Security-Policy header 'default-src self; script-src self nonce-abc123' enforce?
options: A. All content from external sources is cached and served locally
B. Only scripts from the same origin OR with matching nonce are executed — inline scripts without nonce are blocked
C. Scripts are scanned for malware before execution
D. Only HTTPS scripts are allowed from any origin

questions.question: In Burp Suite Intruder, which attack type uses independent wordlists for each payload position, trying every combination?
options: A. Sniper
B. Pitchfork
C. Cluster Bomb
D. Battering Ram

questions.question: What is a CSRF attack and what is the most effective prevention mechanism?
options: A. Injecting scripts into another user's session; prevention: CSP headers
B. Cross-service request flooding targeting APIs; prevention: rate limiting
C. Client-side request faking via XSS; prevention: input sanitization
D. Forging requests from an authenticated user's browser to a target site; prevention: synchronizer token pattern (CSRF token) or SameSite cookie attribute

questions.question: The 'alg: none' JWT vulnerability allows an attacker to do what?
options: A. Set the algorithm to 'none' so the server skips signature verification — forging tokens with arbitrary claims
B. Encrypt the JWT payload to hide user data from the server
C. Use no algorithm to speed up JWT parsing on high-traffic APIs
D. Disable JWT expiration checking on the server

questions.question: What does 'find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null' find on a Linux system during privilege escalation enumeration?
options: A. World-writable files that any user can modify
B. Files with the SUID bit set — run with the file owner's permissions, potential escalation vector
C. Files modified in the last 4000 seconds
D. Executable files with 4000 bytes or more

questions.question: What does TLS Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) guarantee that standard RSA key exchange does NOT?
options: A. That session keys are rotated every 30 minutes automatically
B. That all TLS 1.2 vulnerabilities are automatically patched
C. Compromise of the server's long-term private key cannot decrypt previously captured session traffic — each session uses ephemeral keys
D. Forward compatibility with future TLS versions

questions.question: In the Cyber Kill Chain, which stage involves creating the exploit-laden payload (malicious document, weaponized PDF)?
options: A. Reconnaissance
B. Delivery
C. Command & Control
D. Weaponization

questions.question: What is the MITRE ATT&CK framework and how is it used in practice?
options: A. A knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) used to map attacks, improve detections, and run purple team exercises
B. A regulatory compliance framework for Fortune 500 companies
C. An automated penetration testing tool developed by MITRE
D. A CVE scoring system alternative developed for nation-state attacks

questions.question: What is a 'dangling DNS' record and what attack does it enable?
options: A. A DNS record with an expired TTL that causes slow resolution
B. A CNAME pointing to a deleted external resource — attacker claims that resource and serves malicious content on the victim's subdomain (subdomain takeover)
C. A DNS record that points to an internal IP, exposed externally
D. An MX record pointing to a non-existent mail server

questions.question: What is the difference between black-box, grey-box, and white-box penetration testing?
options: A. Black = external only; grey = internal only; white = cloud environments
B. Black = automated only; grey = hybrid; white = manual only
C. Black = no prior knowledge; grey = partial knowledge (e.g., credentials but no source); white = full knowledge (source code, architecture, credentials)
D. The difference is only in the colour of the test report cover

questions.question: What does 'passive reconnaissance' involve that makes it harder to detect than active recon?
options: A. Running scans at night when security teams are offline
B. Using stealth Nmap scans with timing option -T1
C. Routing all traffic through Tor to hide the attacker's IP
D. Gathering target intelligence from public sources (OSINT, Shodan, crt.sh, LinkedIn) without sending any traffic directly to the target

questions.question: What is Trivy used for in a DevSecOps pipeline?
options: A. Scanning container images, filesystems, and Git repos for CVEs, misconfigurations, and exposed secrets
B. Dynamic application security testing of running web applications
C. Static analysis of Python and JavaScript source code
D. Network port scanning and service fingerprinting

questions.question: In AWS, what security event would GuardDuty detect that CloudTrail alone cannot alert on?
options: A. An IAM user creating a new access key
B. Cryptocurrency mining behavior detected via unusual EC2 network patterns — ML-based anomaly detection beyond raw API call logging
C. An S3 bucket policy being modified to allow public access
D. Root account login from the AWS console

questions.question: What is privilege escalation in penetration testing, and name a common Linux vector?
options: A. Escalating a bug report to the security team; common vector: using Metasploit autopwn
B. Gaining access to a higher-paying tier of cloud services; common vector: API key theft
C. Gaining higher authorization after initial access; common vector: sudo -l to find misconfigured sudo rights, SUID binaries, writable cron jobs, or kernel exploits
D. Increasing Nmap scan speed with -T5; common vector: port 4444

questions.question: What does the OWASP A10:2021 (SSRF) vulnerability enable that makes it especially dangerous in microservice architectures?
options: A. Allows attackers to run arbitrary SQL on the backend database
B. Forces server-side scripts to execute attacker-supplied code
C. Allows stealing of session tokens from other users via the server
D. Server fetches internal service URLs on the attacker's behalf — can pivot to internal APIs, cloud metadata, or other services not exposed externally

questions.question: What does Argon2id's memory-hard design protect against compared to SHA-256 for password hashing?
options: A. Argon2id requires significant RAM to compute — preventing GPU/ASIC parallel cracking attacks that make SHA-256 trivially fast to brute force
B. Argon2id is reversible, making password recovery possible for admins
C. Argon2id hashes are shorter, reducing database storage needs
D. SHA-256 is deprecated; Argon2id is just its replacement with identical security properties

questions.question: What is the purpose of Certificate Transparency (CT) logs, and how can defenders use them?
options: A. CT logs store certificate private keys for disaster recovery
B. CT logs are public, append-only records of all issued TLS certificates — defenders monitor them (via crt.sh, Cert Spotter) to detect unauthorized certificates issued for their domains
C. CT logs enforce certificate revocation across all browsers globally
D. CT logs encrypt certificate metadata to prevent MITM attacks
